symptom: Rotten-egg or sulfur smell near a gas furnace, water heater, or gas line. Customer may report headaches or dizziness if the smell has persisted. Hissing sound possible but not required.
diagnosis: Natural gas leak. This is a life-safety event, not a repair. The mercaptan additive in natural gas smells like rotten eggs at concentrations well below flammability — if you smell it, concentration is significant.
procedure: DO NOT touch any electrical switches, plug or unplug anything, or use a lighter / flame / sparking tool. Static discharge can ignite a gas-air mixture.
Evacuate occupants immediately. Move upwind if outdoors, or to the opposite end of the building if indoors.
From a SAFE distance (across the street or 50+ feet away), call the gas utility emergency line. In the US this is typically 911 or the utility's 24/7 leak line.
Do not return to the building until the utility responder has tested with a combustible-gas indicator and declared it safe.
Once cleared: the tech may investigate the leak source (flare fitting, old black-iron union, cracked heat exchanger). Never reopen the gas valve without a manometer leak test showing pressure hold.
Document the event in the service record. If a heat exchanger crack is the source, the furnace must be red-tagged and replaced — not patched.
safety_notes: This is the ONE KB entry where the correct action is to stop working and evacuate. The app MUST call flag_safety with level='stop' the moment gas smell is reported.
Carbon monoxide is odorless — a gas smell ≠ CO. Both can be present. If anyone feels dizzy, nauseous, or has a headache, treat it as CO exposure and seek fresh air + medical attention.
Never use a match or lighter to 'find' a gas leak. People have died.
